slides.title: Model Context Protocol
slides.subtitle: The open standard that lets AI talk to everything else

slides.name: Aravind Govindhasamy
slides.title: Software Developer
slides.experience: Several years across .NET, Python, IoT, RFID, and modern JavaScript.
slides.oss_experience: Building MCP servers since the spec dropped in late 2024.

slides.title: Why MCP exists
slides.subtitle: A short story about a long-standing pain.

slides.title: The N×M problem
slides.subtitle: Every AI app, every tool, every integration — written from scratch.

box.content:
If you wanted Claude to read your filesystem, you wrote a Claude-specific integration. If you wanted GPT-4 to do the same, you wrote a GPT-specific one. If you wanted both to use the same Postgres database — two more integrations.

N models × M tools = N × M one-off integrations. The whole industry was rewriting the same code with slightly different shapes.

list.text: Every vendor had a slightly different tool-calling API
Every team rebuilt auth, errors, schemas, streaming separately
Switching models meant rewriting every integration
Sharing integrations across companies was impossible
The ecosystem was siloed before it even matured

slides.title: Before vs After MCP
leftPoints: 🔌 Custom adapter per (model, tool) pair
🧱 Auth, errors, schemas reinvented each time
🔒 Integrations locked to one vendor
🐌 Updating a tool means redeploying every client
💔 No shared community of reusable connectors
rightPoints: 🔄 One protocol, any model talks to any server
📦 Auth, errors, schemas — all standardised
🌍 Vendor-neutral by design
⚡ Add a server once, every MCP client gets it
🤝 A growing ecosystem of community servers

note.text: Anthropic called MCP "the USB-C port of AI applications" — and it stuck.

slides.title: What is MCP?
slides.subtitle: An open, vendor-neutral protocol for AI ↔ tool integrations.

box.content:
MCP is an open protocol that standardises how applications provide context to LLMs.

Think of it like LSP (Language Server Protocol) — but for AI tools. One spec, many clients, many servers, no vendor lock-in.

list.text: November 2024: Anthropic released MCP as an open standard
Open source from day one — MIT licensed, on GitHub
2025: OpenAI, Microsoft, Google all announced MCP support
The spec lives at: modelcontextprotocol.io

slides.title: Core concepts
slides.subtitle: Five words you'll see everywhere: client, server, resources, tools, prompts.

box.content: MCP is a client-server protocol. The terminology is precise:

list.text: Host — the AI app a user interacts with (Claude Desktop, Cursor, etc.)
Client — the connection inside a host that talks to one server
Server — exposes resources, tools, and prompts over MCP

box.title: Everything an MCP server exposes falls into one of three buckets:

list.text: Resources — read-only data the model can pull in (files, DB rows, API responses)
Tools — executable functions the model can invoke (with side effects)
Prompts — reusable interaction templates the user can trigger

box.content:
Resources are read-only. They're how a server exposes context to the model — files, rows, configuration, anything addressable by a URI.

The host decides which resources to attach to the conversation. The model never invokes them directly; the user (via the host) does.

Tools are functions with side effects. They can write to a database, send an email, run a command — anything.

The model decides when to call a tool, the host gates execution (often requiring user approval), and the server actually runs it.

Prompts are user-triggered templates. A server can expose curated, parameterised prompts ("summarise this PR", "plan a sprint from these issues") that the user picks from a menu.

They keep complex workflows out of the user's head and inside the integration.

slides.title: Architecture
slides.subtitle: How a request actually travels from user to LLM to tool and back.

slides.title: The full picture
slides.content:
┌──────────────────────────┐       JSON-RPC 2.0        ┌─────────────────────────┐
│       MCP Host           │ ◄──────────────────────► │       MCP Server         │
│  (Claude Desktop, Cursor,│        stdio / HTTP+SSE   │  (filesystem, github,    │
│   Zed, Cline, custom...) │                           │   postgres, slack, ...)  │
└────────────┬─────────────┘                           └────────────┬────────────┘
             │                                                       │
             │ calls LLM with                                        │ exposes
             │ tool results + context                                │ resources / tools / prompts
             ▼                                                       ▼
       ┌──────────┐                                          ┌────────────────┐
       │   LLM    │                                          │    Backend     │
       │ (Claude, │                                          │  (DB, API,     │
       │  GPT-4,  │                                          │   files, SaaS) │
       │  Gemini) │                                          └────────────────┘
       └──────────┘

One host can connect to many servers. One server can be reused across many hosts.
The LLM never talks to the server directly — the host orchestrates everything.

note.text: The host is the trust boundary. The server doesn't trust the model; the model doesn't trust the server; the user trusts the host.

box.content: Every MCP message is a JSON-RPC 2.0 request, response, or notification. Familiar, boring, well-tooled — exactly what you want for a protocol.

The first thing a client and server do is negotiate capabilities. The client tells the server what it supports; the server tells the client what it offers.

This is how MCP stays forward-compatible: optional features (sampling, subscriptions, progress) are only enabled when both sides agree.

list.text: Initialize — exchange protocol version, server info, capabilities
Operate — list/read resources, list/call tools, list/get prompts
Shutdown — graceful close on either side

slides.title: Transports
slides.subtitle: MCP is transport-agnostic. Pick the one that matches your deployment.

box.content:
The host launches the server as a child process and talks to it over stdin/stdout. Newline-delimited JSON-RPC messages.

Use it when the server runs on the user's machine — filesystem access, local databases, CLI tools, anything with strong local-trust assumptions.

The server runs as a long-lived HTTP service. The host POSTs requests; the server streams responses via SSE.

Use it when the server is a hosted service, when one server needs to serve many users, or when you want centralised logging/auth.

Introduced in 2025, Streamable HTTP combines request/response and server-push into a single HTTP endpoint. Simpler to deploy than SSE, friendlier to load balancers, and what most new remote servers use.

slides.title: stdio vs HTTP — which to pick
leftPoints: 💻 The server runs on the user's machine
📁 You need local filesystem / OS access
🔒 Auth is just "the user launched this process"
🚀 Zero-config setup matters most
🧪 You're prototyping a server
rightPoints: ☁️ The server is a hosted service
👥 Many users share one server
🔑 You need real auth (OAuth, API keys)
📊 You want centralised observability
🌍 The server is internet-facing

slides.title: Resources — deeper look
slides.subtitle: Read-only context the user attaches to a conversation.

box.content: Two primary endpoints:

list.text: resources/list — server returns the menu of available resources (with URIs, MIME types, names)
resources/read — given a URI, server returns the content (text, blob, or binary)

box.content:
Servers can optionally support resources/subscribe. When the underlying data changes, the server pushes a notification to the client — which can re-read.

This is how a live document or a streaming log feels real-time to the model, without polling.

slides.title: Tools — deeper look
slides.subtitle: The model can call these. The user approves them. The server runs them.

box.content:
tools/list returns every tool the server offers, each with a name, a human-readable description, and a JSON Schema describing its inputs.

The host hands that schema to the LLM. The model uses standard tool-calling to decide when to invoke a tool — but goes through MCP to actually do it.

The model emits a tool call. The host validates the user has consented. The host sends tools/call to the server with the tool name + arguments. The server runs the work, returns content, and the host hands it back to the model as the next turn.

slides.title: Prompts — deeper look
slides.subtitle: Slash-commands for AI: curated workflows the server exposes.

box.content:
Tools are model-driven. Resources are user-driven. Prompts are user-triggered but server-authored — the integration packages a complex interaction into a one-click flow.

Examples: "Summarise this PR", "Plan a sprint from these tickets", "Audit this Postgres query for safety".

slides.title: Sampling
slides.subtitle: The server-side superpower most people miss.

box.content:
Normally, only the host calls the LLM. Sampling reverses that direction: an MCP server can ask the host's LLM to complete a prompt on the server's behalf.

The host stays in control — it sees the request, can deny it, can transform it. But the server gets to use the model as part of its own logic.

list.text: An agent server that wants to do multi-step reasoning between tool calls
A research server that asks the LLM to summarise a long doc before returning it
A code server that wants the LLM's help generating a patch
Anywhere the server needs LLM capability without bringing its own API key

slides.title: Quick check
slides.subtitle: Three questions to make sure the model is loaded into your context.

slides.question: Which MCP primitive is read-only and user-attached, not model-invoked?
slides.explanation: Resources are read-only context. The user (via the host) decides what to attach. Tools are model-invoked, prompts are user-triggered templates, sampling is server → host LLM calls.

slides.question: Which transport is best for a server that runs locally on the user's machine?
slides.explanation: stdio is the standard local transport — the host launches the server as a child process. HTTP+SSE is for hosted/remote servers. WebSocket and gRPC are not first-class MCP transports.

slides.question: What does "sampling" mean in MCP?
options: The server returns a random subset of resources
The model picks a tool at random
An MCP server asks the host's LLM to complete a prompt on its behalf
The protocol samples bytes for compression
slides.explanation: Sampling reverses the usual direction — instead of the host calling the LLM, the server can request an LLM completion through the host. The host stays in control of policy and cost.

slides.title: Building your first server
slides.subtitle: From npm install to working tool in under 30 lines.

box.content: Anthropic and the community maintain official SDKs in:

list.text: TypeScript — @modelcontextprotocol/sdk (most popular)
Python — mcp
Java — official, used by Spring AI
Kotlin, C#, Swift, Rust — all maintained

slides.title: The ecosystem
slides.subtitle: Who's already shipping with MCP, and which servers you can plug in today.

box.title: Maintained by Anthropic and the core team:

list.text: filesystem — read/write files inside a sandboxed root
github — issues, PRs, files, search
postgres — schema introspection + read-only queries
slack — channels, messages, users
memory — a persistent knowledge graph the agent maintains
brave-search — web search
puppeteer / browser — page navigation and scraping
Claude Desktop — the original and reference host
Cursor, Cline, Zed, Continue.dev — IDE integrations
OpenAI — added MCP support in 2025
Microsoft Copilot Studio — MCP-compatible since 2025
Claude Code — the CLI you may be reading this in
Notion, Linear, Jira, ClickUp, Airtable
MySQL, MongoDB, SQLite, Snowflake, BigQuery
AWS, GCP, Azure read-only inspectors
Gmail, Outlook, Discord, Telegram
Docker, Kubernetes, Stripe, Cloudflare

box.content: Companies that have publicly talked about MCP in production:

list.text: Block (Square) — internal coding assistants
Apollo — research workflows
Replit — agent infrastructure
Sourcegraph — Cody integrations
Codeium, Zed, Cursor — IDE tool integration

slides.title: Security & user consent
slides.subtitle: The host is the trust boundary. Design accordingly.

box.content: MCP doesn't let servers do silent side-effects. The protocol assumes the host will prompt the user before any tool runs — at least the first time.

list.text: Hosts surface tool descriptions to the user
Hosts can block dangerous tools by policy
Users can revoke a server's permissions at any time
Auth (OAuth, API keys) is the server's responsibility, not the protocol's

slides.title: MCP vs the alternatives
slides.subtitle: There's a reason MCP is winning. Two reasons. Three reasons.

slides.title: MCP vs vendor function calling
leftPoints: 🔒 Tied to one model provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google)
🧱 Schemas defined per app, not shared across apps
🚫 No standard for resources or prompts — tools only
🪨 You rebuild integrations when you switch models
🤷 No community library of reusable connectors
rightPoints: 🌐 Vendor-neutral — works with any model that supports tool use
📦 One schema, every MCP host understands it
🎯 Tools, resources, and prompts as first-class primitives
♻️ Switch models without rewriting your integrations
🤝 Growing ecosystem of community servers

slides.title: MCP vs framework-level tool libraries
leftPoints: 🐍 Python-first, often library-specific
🧪 Tools live inside your app's process
🔗 Hard to share tools across teams or apps
🪦 Tied to one framework's lifecycle
🧱 Not a protocol — an SDK
rightPoints: 🌍 Polyglot — TypeScript, Python, Java, Kotlin, C#, etc.
🚪 Servers run as separate processes — clean trust boundary
♻️ Same server reusable across every MCP-aware app
📜 A spec, not a library — survives framework churn
🤝 Designed for multi-vendor, multi-process worlds

note.text: LangChain and MCP aren't mutually exclusive — many MCP servers are built using LangChain internally.

slides.title: Getting started today
slides.subtitle: Three things you can do this afternoon.

list.text: Install Claude Desktop and configure one official server (filesystem is the easiest)
Clone the SDK examples at github.com/modelcontextprotocol — run a hello-world server
Build your own server for whatever tool you wish your AI could already use. Publish it.

slides.title: Thank you
slides.message: MCP is one of those rare moments where the standard arrived early enough to actually matter. Get in on it.
